skill.name: senior-rust-practices
skill.description: Guides Rust developers on workspace architecture, crate boundaries, dependency hygiene, testing strategy, logging, CI gates, and production project structure.
skill.summary: Apply senior Rust project practices for scalable workspaces, clean crate boundaries, and reliable quality gates.
skill.author: ClementWalter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and weak-cryptography patterns, but review found they are Markdown inline code, code fences, or ordinary prose. The only confirmed concern is that the skill recommends shell commands and a hidden-home worktree path; this is transparent developer workflow guidance, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Developer Shell Commands in Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill recommends running Cargo, trunk, cargo-deny, cargo-audit, cargo-fuzz, and CI commands. These are expected for a Rust development skill, but users should review commands before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible in Markdown and are standard Rust quality and audit tooling. I found no evidence that they execute automatically or send data externally.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Worktree Path
low_findings.description: The skill suggests creating a git worktree below a hidden home directory path. This modifies local filesystem state but is explicit and related to the stated development workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 14 contains a concrete shell command with a hidden path and command substitution. The intent appears to be workspace organization, not concealment or persistence.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Ruby Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer interpreted many inline Markdown backticks and fenced code blocks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is documentation content and does not contain executable Ruby code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown examples or inline documentation. There is no script file, interpreter directive, or execution wrapper.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords
dangerous_patterns.description: Weak-cryptography alerts were triggered by ordinary words such as description, design, dependencies, and deliberate. No weak algorithm use was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain prose about Rust design and dependencies. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic implementation guidance at these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Rust Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: Reconnaissance alerts map to benign Rust project guidance about tests, features, workspace members, and visibility. No system probing or network scanning behavior was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is developer documentation and architecture advice. I found no commands that enumerate hosts, ports, users, credentials, or system configuration.

content.value_statement: Rust teams often struggle with crate boundaries, dependencies, and quality gates as projects grow. This skill provides practical guidance for workspace design, testing, logging, and production structure.
actual_capabilities: Recommends Rust workspace layouts for multi-crate projects.
Explains crate boundaries for core, adapters, app, and binary layers.
Guides dependency management with workspace dependencies and feature flags.
Defines testing strategies across unit, integration, end-to-end, property, and doctest layers.
Suggests tracing, clippy, formatting, testing, and audit quality gates.
Advises on project patterns for CLIs, services, crypto code, and WASM.
limitations: It provides guidance only and does not inspect a repository by itself.
Some commands require Rust tooling that may not be installed.
Version recommendations use broad latest-version examples, not pinned releases.
It does not replace a security review of project-specific unsafe code.

use_cases.title: Design a Rust Workspace
use_cases.description: Plan crates, dependency direction, and module boundaries before a project becomes difficult to reorganize.

use_cases.title: Raise Project Quality Gates
use_cases.description: Add practical formatting, linting, testing, dependency audit, and CI checks for an existing Rust codebase.

use_cases.title: Review Architecture Choices
use_cases.description: Evaluate whether core logic, IO adapters, async runtime code, and public APIs are separated clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Workspace
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new Rust project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Rust workspace for a small service with a CLI, domain logic, storage, and HTTP API.

prompt_templates.title: Organize Existing Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate refactor of a growing repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Rust project structure and suggest crate and module boundaries that reduce coupling.

prompt_templates.title: Define Quality Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Rust CI quality gate plan with formatting, clippy, tests, dependency audit, and MSRV checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Architecture Tradeoffs
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this Rust workspace for dependency leakage, async placement, feature flag design, and testing coverage.

output_examples.input: How should I split a Rust service into crates?
output_examples.output: A concise crate layout with core, adapters, app, and binary layers, plus dependency direction rules.

output_examples.input: What checks should my Rust CI run?
output_examples.output: A staged quality gate plan covering formatting, clippy, workspace tests, dependency audits, and optional coverage.

output_examples.input: My library uses anyhow everywhere. Is that fine?
output_examples.output: A review explaining when typed errors are better for libraries and where opaque errors still fit.

best_practices: Keep pure domain logic separate from filesystem, database, network, and runtime code.
Centralize dependency versions while preserving clear architectural boundaries.
Use focused tests at the right layer and reserve end-to-end tests for important scenarios.
anti_patterns: Splitting every type into its own crate before real boundaries exist.
Letting core crates depend on async runtimes, HTTP clients, or database libraries.
Using broad public APIs that expose internals and make refactoring harder.

faq.question: Does this skill write Rust code?
faq.answer: It mainly guides architecture, testing, dependency choices, and workflow decisions. It can support code changes when paired with an editing agent.

faq.question: Is it only for large Rust projects?
faq.answer: No. It recommends starting small, then splitting crates only when boundaries and maintenance needs justify it.

faq.question: Can it help with Cargo workspaces?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains workspace members, shared dependencies, feature flags, and common multi-crate layouts.

faq.question: Does it cover Rust testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, property tests, fuzzing, and doctests.

faq.question: Does it replace cargo-audit or cargo-deny?
faq.answer: No. It recommends those tools but does not perform dependency vulnerability or license analysis by itself.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production Rust services?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on boundaries, dependency hygiene, tracing, CI gates, and project patterns used in production systems.
